free_text: The ORNL Fast-Chopper Time-of-Flight Neutron
Spectrometer
1-in BF3 chambers, 0.75-m diameter
liquid scintillation tank.
BP
OS. SF9=DERIV added in subs.2 and 3.
OS. Correction in sub.2
This installation has an over all flight path of
180 m long with intermediate detector stations at
approximately 12, 25, and 45 m. The neutrons are
detected at the end of flight path by BF3
proportional counters. In this measurement six 10BF3
proportional counters were located at the 12- or 45-m
flight stations.
In order to determine the 2200 m/sec cross sections
for these nuclides the experimental data were fitted
by the least-squares method to the following
equation: [sigma(total) - sigma(sc)] = a/sqrt(E) + b,
where sigma(total), sigma(sc) are respectively the
total and scattering cross sections, E is the neutron
energy, and a and b are the coefficients of fit.
No information on source of uncertainties.
page 543.

Fast chopper 1.95 x 10**21 atoms/cm2.
Authors say, in general, though,
these chopper data are to be regarded as preliminary
and good to ~20-25%.
Fig. 8, page 544.
A tick foil of the fissionable material is
placed at the 45-m flight station and the fast
neutrons are detected by either a Hornyak button or
by a liquid scintillator with a neutron/gamma-ray
discrimination circuit. The 10BF3 detector measures
the incident neutron flux. This method is used only
to measure the relative value of eta as a function of
energy; the data are normalized to the thermal value
which has been obtained by other workers.
No information on source of uncertainties.
Authors say that data are preliminary in nature.
Fig. 12, page 547.
